message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="lea-verou" color="pink" summary="Frontend/CSS agreements for Day 0 team discussion">
Hey Kent! Lea here. Really excited about this project -- a todo list app is deceptively simple, and that's what makes it a perfect canvas for doing things *right* with the web platform.

Here are my proposed agreements for the frontend layer:

## 1. Progressive Enhancement Layers

The app should work in three layers, each one enhancing the previous:

- **Layer 1 (HTML)**: The page is functional with just semantic HTML. Forms submit, links navigate, content is readable. No CSS, no JS required for core functionality.
- **Layer 2 (CSS)**: Makes it beautiful -- layout, typography, color, spacing, transitions, focus styles. This is where the bulk of the "frontend" work lives.
- **Layer 3 (TypeScript)**: Adds delight -- drag-and-drop reordering, keyboard shortcuts beyond native behavior, optimistic UI hints. HTMX handles the server-driven interactivity, so TypeScript is only for things *neither HTML nor HTMX* can do.

**Agreement**: Before writing TypeScript for any visual or interactive behavior, we check whether CSS can handle it. CSS can do transitions, animations, hover/focus states, dark mode, responsive layout, toggle states (via `:checked`, `:has()`), and much more.

## 2. CSS Architecture

I propose a **custom properties-first** design token system:

```css
:root {
  /* Spacing scale */
  --space-xs: 0.25rem;
  --space-sm: 0.5rem;
  --space-md: 1rem;
  --space-lg: 1.5rem;
  --space-xl: 2rem;

  /* Colors in oklch for perceptual uniformity */
  --color-primary: oklch(55% 0.2 260);
  --color-surface: oklch(98% 0.005 260);
  --color-text: oklch(20% 0.02 260);
  /* etc. */

  /* Typography */
  --font-body: system-ui, sans-serif;
  --font-size-base: 1rem;
  --line-height-base: 1.5;

  /* Shadows, radii, etc. */
}
```

**Why oklch?** It gives us perceptually uniform lightness, so we can programmatically generate color scales, ensure consistent contrast, and do dark mode by shifting the lightness channel. `color-mix()` in oklch is incredible for this.

**Agreement**: All "magic numbers" for colors, spacing, typography, and shadows go through custom properties. No hardcoded hex values or pixel values scattered through component styles.

## 3. CSS Organization with Cascade Layers

I strongly recommend using `@layer` for CSS organization:

```css
@layer reset, tokens, base, layout, components, utilities;
```

This gives us explicit control over the cascade without specificity wars. Each layer has a clear purpose:
- **reset**: Normalize/reset styles
- **tokens**: Custom properties (design tokens)
- **base**: Element-level styles (body, headings, links, forms)
- **layout**: Page-level layout (Grid for overall structure)
- **components**: Component-specific styles (todo-item, auth-form, etc.)
- **utilities**: Small helper classes (visually-hidden, etc.)

**Agreement**: We use cascade layers. No `!important` unless it's in the utilities layer for intentional overrides.

## 4. Semantic HTML Standards

- Use `<button>` for actions, `<a>` for navigation -- never `<div onclick>`
- Use `<form>` for all data submission (works with both standard submission and HTMX)
- Use `<dialog>` for modals (native browser behavior, accessible by default)
- Use `<details>/<summary>` for disclosure widgets where appropriate
- Use `<main>`, `<nav>`, `<header>`, `<footer>` for landmark regions
- Headings (`<h1>`-`<h6>`) must follow a logical hierarchy

**Agreement**: We validate semantic HTML as part of our review process. Heydon and I will be particularly attentive to this.

## 5. Modern CSS Features We Should Use

- **`:has()` selector**: Perfect for this app! `li:has(input:checked)` lets us style completed todos without any JavaScript class toggling.
- **Container queries**: Components should respond to their container size, not just viewport. This makes them truly reusable.
- **Logical properties**: Use `margin-inline`, `padding-block`, etc. instead of physical directions. Future-proofs for RTL/internationalization.
- **`clamp()` for fluid typography**: `font-size: clamp(1rem, 0.5rem + 1vw, 1.25rem)` -- responsive text without media queries.
- **View Transitions API**: For smooth transitions when HTMX swaps content.

**Agreement**: We prefer modern CSS features with `@supports` fallbacks where needed. Our target is modern evergreen browsers (last 2 versions of Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge).

## 6. TypeScript Conventions

When TypeScript IS needed (drag-and-drop, keyboard shortcuts):

- **No framework**: Vanilla TypeScript with DOM APIs
- **Progressive enhancement pattern**: The feature must work without JS (e.g., reorder via form submission), then JS enhances it
- **Event delegation**: Attach listeners to parent containers, not individual elements
- **Thin layer**: TypeScript files should be small and focused. If a TS file is getting large, we're probably doing something CSS could handle.
- **No CSS-in-JS**: Styles live in CSS files. TypeScript can toggle classes or data attributes, but never sets inline styles (except for computed values like drag position).

**Agreement**: TypeScript toggles state via `data-*` attributes or classes; CSS handles all visual changes based on those attributes.

## 7. Browser Compatibility

- Target: last 2 versions of evergreen browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge)
- Use `@supports` for newer features (container queries, `:has()`, view transitions)
- No IE11 support (it's 2026, we're free!)
- Test in at least Chrome and Firefox during development

**Agreement**: We define a browserslist and use it consistently. No polyfills unless absolutely necessary -- graceful degradation via `@supports` is preferred.

## 8. How Design Tokens Flow

Steve Schoger defines the visual design -> We encode it as CSS custom properties -> Components reference only custom properties -> Dark mode / themes just reassign the properties.

This means the design system is entirely in CSS. No build step for tokens. No JSON-to-CSS pipeline. Just `:root` custom properties that Steve and I maintain together.

---

I think these agreements set us up for a clean, maintainable, standards-first frontend. The big themes are: **use the platform**, **CSS-first**, and **semantic HTML always**.

Happy to discuss any of these! Particularly excited to show the team what `:has()` and container queries can do for this todo list app.
</teammate-message>

content.text: Lea's input is thorough and technically precise. She and Steve Schoger are already naturally aligned on the design token flow. Let me acknowledge her contribution.

input.content:
Lea, this is excellent. You've taken the CSS section of the draft and given it real teeth -- specific techniques, clear organization, and a principled approach to the cascade. The level of detail here is exactly what will prevent "but how do we actually do this?" conversations in the mob.

Let me map your input:

**Already represented (but you've made it much more concrete):**
- CSS custom properties for design tokens (Section 3)
- CSS-first, JS-last principle (Section 3, CSS conventions)
- Semantic HTML standards (Section 3, HTML/HTMX conventions)
- Modern layout techniques (Section 3)

**Needs to be added or strengthened:**
1. **Three-layer progressive enhancement model** (HTML -> CSS -> TypeScript) -- This is a clearer articulation than what's in the draft. It connects beautifully with Carson's progressive enhancement requirement and Heydon's accessibility concerns.
2. **`@layer` for CSS organization** -- The six-layer cascade structure (reset, tokens, base, layout, components, utilities) is concrete and practical. I'll add this to the code conventions. The "no `!important`" rule is a good constraint.
3. **oklch color space** -- This is a specific technical decision worth capturing. The perceptual uniformity argument is compelling, especially for generating consistent contrast ratios (which Heydon will care about).
4. **`:has()` for todo completion styling** -- Great concrete example. `li:has(input:checked)` means zero JavaScript for a core visual behavior. That's the platform doing the work.
5. **TypeScript conventions** -- Your "thin layer" rules (no framework, event delegation, data attributes for state, CSS handles visuals) are precise and will prevent scope creep into JS-land.
6. **Browser compatibility target** -- Last 2 versions of evergreen browsers, `@supports` for graceful degradation. Practical and clear.
7. **Design token flow** -- Steve Schoger defines -> you encode as custom properties -> components reference only tokens -> themes reassign properties. No build step. Clean.

**Connections I'm seeing across the team:**
- Your `:has()` approach means Carson gets his "no client-side state" wish -- CSS reads the DOM state directly.
- Your oklch colors give Heydon reliable contrast ratios.
- Your `@layer` structure gives Steve Schoger's design tokens a clear home.
- Your progressive enhancement layers formalize what Carson and Heydon both need.

One question: On container queries vs viewport queries -- do you see us using container queries extensively for this todo app, or is it more of a "use them where they make sense" agreement? I want to set the right expectation for the team.

I'll incorporate all of this in the next revision. You and Steve Schoger clearly share a mental model on the design token flow, which is great -- that partnership will be smooth.
input.summary: Acknowledged Lea's CSS architecture and web standards input

5. **TypeScript conventions** -- Your "thin layer" rules (no framework, event delegation, data attributes for state, CSS handles visuals) are precise and will prevent scope creep into JS-land.
6. **Browser compatibility target** -- Last 2 versions of evergreen browsers, `@supports` for graceful degradation. Practical and clear.
7. **Design token flow** -- Steve Schoger defines -> you encode as custom properties -> components reference only tokens -> themes reassign properties. No build step. Clean.

**Connections I'm seeing across the team:**
- Your `:has()` approach means Carson gets his "no client-side state" wish -- CSS reads the DOM state directly.

I'll incorporate all of this in the next revision. You and Steve Schoger clearly share a mental model on the design token flow, which is great -- that partnership will be smooth."
  }

content.text: Six teammates have now provided input: Marty, Carson, Scott, Steve Schoger, Steve Krug, and Lea. Still waiting on Luca (Rust engineering) and Heydon (accessibility). I have very strong coverage now. Once I hear from those two -- or if there's a natural pause -- I'll do the consolidated revision of the TEAM_AGREEMENTS.md.
